food_subgroup: Tortilla-based snack dishes with meat and cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers nachos as a category; no specific beef-topped nacho entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Corn chips / tortilla chips — used as closest parent for nacho-based dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific beef nachos composite entry. A0CKP (corn/tortilla chips) is the closest defensible base-ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for beef nachos or nachos as a dish category.

preparation_method: baked or fried tortilla chips as base
seasoned ground beef cooked on stovetop
allergens: gluten (corn chips may contain traces; cheese sauce may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of beef nachos is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a shared platter of tortilla chips topped with seasoned ground beef, melted cheese, jalapeños, and salsa.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos with meat and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for beef nachos as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy entries: tortilla chips (FDC 172431), cooked ground beef 80/20 (FDC 174036), cheddar cheese (FDC 173414), jalapeños (FDC 169392), and tomato salsa (FDC 170071), weighted to a representative restaurant-style ratio of approximately 40% chips, 30% beef, 20% cheese, 5% jalapeños, 5% salsa by weight. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry 'Nachos, with beef and cheese' (FDC 171958) where available.

gi_source.title: Corn chips / tortilla chips GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GL estimated from serving carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef nachos as a composite dish. GI of 63 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn tortilla chips (GI ~63, medium), which constitutes the primary glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The beef, cheese, and salsa components contribute negligible additional glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 54.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef nachos due to the high sodium load from seasoned chips, processed cheese, and salsa, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the 2000 mg recommended limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-GI refined corn carbohydrate base with high glycemic load per serving (~54 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume beef nachos only occasionally and in small portions, as the large carbohydrate load from corn tortilla chips produces a high glycemic load per restaurant serving that can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.9 g/100 g) and sodium (~420 mg/100 g) from beef, cheese, and chips
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit beef nachos due to the combined burden of saturated fat from ground beef and cheese and high sodium from chips and salsa, both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef nachos is a popular Tex-Mex dish consisting of a base of crispy corn tortilla chips generously topped with seasoned ground beef, melted cheese (typically cheddar or a processed cheese sauce), sliced jalapeños, and tomato salsa. The dish is often finished with sour cream, guacamole, or fresh coriander. In restaurant settings, beef nachos are typically served as a shared starter or main course on a large platter. The texture profile combines the crunch of the chips with the soft, savoury beef and the creamy, molten cheese. The flavour is bold, salty, and mildly spicy from the jalapeños. Nutritionally, beef nachos are energy-dense (~270 kcal per 100 g), with a significant carbohydrate contribution from the corn chips, moderate protein from the beef, and notable fat from both the beef and cheese. Sodium content is high, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~63) combined with the large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving results in a high glycemic load, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, beef nachos are served at Mexican and Tex-Mex restaurants and are available on food delivery platforms such as field survey.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in beef nachos?
What is the GI of nachos?
How much sodium is in beef nachos?
Are nachos high in saturated fat?
Is beef nachos suitable for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for beef nachos as a restaurant-style composite dish was found. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy component entries and cross-checked against the closest available USDA composite entry (FDC 171958). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn tortilla chips as the primary glycemic component. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA component entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at the level of precision required. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA composite reference used. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ground beef and chip component entries. The halal flag is set to false as beef nachos served at the referenced Singapore restaurants cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without direct restaurant verification. The cuisine tag 'Naan' in the input record is not relevant to beef nachos and has been disregarded as a data artefact from the field survey seed record. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla chips per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for beef nachos as a composite dish was located in the approved GI source database.
